# Architecture 2.0 Resource Roles and Missing Infrastructure {#sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This appendix provides an actionable rubric for judging resources by their loop role, identifies the shared infrastructure the field still lacks, and marks the boundary between stable book guidance and the living registry of fast-moving links. A resource is useful only when it makes some part of the loop more representable, actionable, measurable, rejectable, or maintainable.}
```

This catalog is not a directory of links and it is not an endorsement list.
Links change, tools age, and benchmark versions move. The stable question is
what role a resource plays in the design loop. Does it provide workload state?
Does it define valid actions? Does it return feedback? Does it expose evidence
that can reject a candidate? Does it preserve provenance, failed runs, and
rejected alternatives?

The specific examples named below are a snapshot. The durable content is the
role each resource plays; the current list of tools, datasets, and benchmarks is
the kind of fast-moving record that belongs with the community forming around
this topic, where it can stay current without reprinting the book. This appendix
keeps the stable framework for judging whether a resource belongs in the loop
and points readers to the live registry for the moving list. Named tools,
datasets, and benchmarks that appear in the catalog without a bibliography
citation, such as OpenROAD (an RTL-to-GDS flow), MLCommons (an engineering consortium), VerilogEval (a hardware code generation benchmark), and CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems), are tracked with
current links in that registry rather than the reference list.

> **Architecture 2.0 resource.** A resource is useful for Architecture 2.0
> when it makes some part of the design loop explicit, such as task, representation,
> environment, method role, feedback, evidence, rejection, decision ownership,
> or declared automation authority.

A first-pass resource-role rubric appears in
@tbl-resource-catalog-loop-roles. The examples are representative rather than
exhaustive. Read each row as an adoption test. Name what the resource carries,
what failure it makes visible, and what claim it cannot support. A synthesis
wrapper that still requires manual log interpretation, for example, is missing
a parsed feedback channel even if it exposes a tool API.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{22\baselineskip}
```

| **Resource family** | **Representative examples** | **What it can carry** | **Adoption boundary** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Architecture corpora and QA | Paper/manual corpora, publication records derived from DBLP (a computer science bibliography database), and QA (question answering) and reasoning data modeled after QuArch (an architecture question-answering dataset) [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch; @PrakashEtAl2025QuArchReasoning]. | Extract claim boundaries, architecture vocabulary, missing loop fields, and literature-grounded evidence limits; do not treat paper text as simulator state or rejection authority. | Paper text rarely preserves simulator state, failed candidates, tool logs, or review judgment. |
| Workloads and benchmarks | XRBench (a mobile extended reality benchmark), MLPerf (an industry-standard machine learning benchmark), and maintained benchmark suites [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf; @ReddiEtAl2020MLPerfInference]. | Define workload state, scenarios, metrics, rules, and comparability. | Coverage, drift, update policy, and proxy validity must remain visible. |
| Evaluation harnesses and environments | ArchGym-style environments (such as an open-source gymnasium for architecture search), benchmark harnesses, simulator wrappers, and tool-calling APIs [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. | Define valid actions, observations, feedback cost, logging, and rejection behavior. | A wrapper can hide tool semantics, unsupported actions, nondeterminism, and failure modes. |
| Mapping and DSE frameworks | Timeloop and MAESTRO-style mapping and dataflow tools (analytical models for accelerator evaluation) [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop; @KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO]. | Make architecture search spaces and constraints explicit enough to explore. | Fast feedback is still a model; calibration, workload scope, and invalid candidates matter. |
| Compiler, autotuning, and codegen resources | AutoTVM and Ansor (tensor program optimizers), MLIR (a compiler infrastructure), and kernel-generation benchmarks [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor; @LattnerEtAl2020MLIR; @OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench]. | Connect specialized hardware ideas to executable software paths. | A kernel, schedule, or IR (intermediate representation) result is not automatically a system-level architecture result. |
| RTL (register-transfer level) and verification benchmarks | Executable specification-to-RTL and Verilog design-problem tasks such as VerilogEval and CVDP (Comprehensive Verilog Design Problems) (datasets for hardware code generation); see the living registry. | Test method claims against compile, simulation, and verification feedback. | Passing a small HDL (hardware description language) task is not the same as closing an architecture loop. |
| Full-system simulation and hardware/software harnesses | gem5 (a modular full-system simulator), FireSim (an FPGA-accelerated simulation platform), Chipyard (an integrated SoC [system-on-chip] design framework), and related harnesses [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5; @KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim; @chipyard]. | Connect workload execution, software stacks, generated hardware, and stronger feedback. | Setup state, versions, nondeterminism, and workload coverage must be recorded. |
| Physical-design and EDA (electronic design automation) evidence | OpenROAD's open RTL-to-GDS flow, CircuitNet (an open-source dataset for machine learning in EDA), ChiPBench (a benchmark for physical design), and placement or signoff-adjacent artifacts [@ChaiEtAl2022CircuitNet; @WangEtAl2025ChiPBench]. | Give physical constraints authority to reject architecture candidates. | Intermediate scores can mislead unless tied to downstream timing, area, power, or routability. |
| Benchmark governance and roadmaps | MLCommons rules, SPEC (Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation) suites, and roadmaps in the style of the International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS). | Maintain comparability, versioning, scenario definitions, and long-horizon evidence needs. | Governance is part of the loop; stale rules or hidden updates change what claims mean. |
| Evidence and provenance artifacts | Design-loop cards, released card or receipt schemas, source records, seeds, configs, tool logs, calibration records, integrity manifests, disclosure boundaries, telemetry packets, failed-run records, and rejected alternatives. | Make claims inspectable and rejectable; suitable runnable receipts can additionally support replay or reproduction. | A schema or directory shape does not establish that the underlying evidence is complete, independent, or available. |

: **Use resource role as the adoption rubric.** A dataset, benchmark, harness, simulator, compiler, or card is valuable only when the loop records what it carries, what failure it exposes, and what it cannot support. {#tbl-resource-catalog-loop-roles tbl-colwidths="[19,25,26,20]"}

For a proposed resource, record its card field, version and owner, exposed
actions or feedback, visible failure modes, and maximum supported claim. An
empty role does not make the loop useless; it narrows the claim. The complete
bootstrap checklist belongs to @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping, while
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card owns the full card and review rubric. This
appendix evaluates resources that can fill those fields rather than defining a
third loop checklist.

## Missing Infrastructure

@sec-what-architect-owns argues that shared evidence infrastructure must make
claims comparable, reviewable, teachable, and, when promised, replayable or
reproducible. @tbl-field-agenda-infrastructure turns that argument into a
concrete inventory.

A repository and an adjudication institution are not the same resource. A
repository can store datasets, code, reports, and failed runs. It does not by
itself define neutral baselines, control conflicts of interest, grant access to
protected evidence, audit accepts and rejects, retire obsolete versions, or
decide what resolves a disagreement. A community laboratory, consortium, or
federated protocol can perform those functions when its ownership, rules,
versions, disclosure boundaries, and appeal path are explicit. The protocol may
use a repository, but the repository cannot substitute for the protocol.

| **Missing infrastructure** | **Minimum concrete contents** | **Capability added** | **Failure if absent** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Paired, versioned multi-fidelity design trajectories | The same workload and candidate IDs carried from proxy through simulation, synthesis, measurement, or silicon; tool and model versions; costs; uncertainty; failed stages; and alignment between levels. | Calibrates where cheap feedback predicts stronger evidence and where escalation is necessary. | Isolated endpoint datasets cannot show when a proxy stopped tracking the design. |
| Benchmark and workload packets | Versioned tasks, scenarios, input distributions, trace provenance, coverage and exclusions, metrics, baselines, validity checks, and an update policy. | Makes task scope, drift, and comparison rules portable across methods and environments. | A method optimizes an ambiguous or stale slice and reports a broader claim. |
| Neutral shared evaluation and adjudication paths | Published intake rules, fixed comparison tasks, accepted baselines, independent execution or authorized review, matched audits of accepts and rejects, conflict disclosures, escalation, and appeal. | Lets a community compare methods and settle bounded disputed claims under shared rules. | A repository collects competing artifacts, but no accepted process can adjudicate them. |
| Versioned environment and tool contracts | Action and observation schemas, valid and invalid states, cost and fidelity labels, tool versions, dependencies, nondeterminism controls, failure codes, and deprecation rules. | Makes method behavior comparable and tool drift diagnosable. | Wrappers hide changed semantics, unsupported actions, or failures that alter the result. |
| Evidence and replay packaging | Claim-to-evidence mapping, configs, seeds, commands, logs, failed runs, rejected alternatives, integrity and disclosure manifests, decision records, and runnable receipts when replay is claimed. | Supports inspection by default and replay or reproduction when the released packet is sufficient. | A plausible result has no reviewable path back to the task, execution, or decision. |
| Governance, maintenance, and education | Named maintainers, version and retirement policy, ownership transfer, contribution and review credit, update cadence, teaching cases, and executable labs. | Keeps infrastructure current, legitimate, and usable by new contributors. | Links decay, tacit practice stays private, and benchmarks outlive their validity. |

: **Missing infrastructure needs artifacts and institutions.** Paired trajectories, workload packets, tool contracts, and replay packages make evidence portable; neutral adjudication, governance, maintenance, and education make it cumulative. {#tbl-field-agenda-infrastructure tbl-colwidths="[20,34,22,18]"}

Not every project needs every row, but a missing row bounds the strongest claim
the loop can support. The living registry can track current implementations of
these roles. It cannot make missing governance or adjudication capacity exist.

## Stable Book Material and Executable Practice

The stable material stays in the book because its role does not depend on a
current tool URL.

- **Bootstrap playbook.** @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping owns the sequence for
  starting a bounded loop.
- **Design-loop card and review rubric.** @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card and
  @tbl-blank-design-loop-card provide the reusable review artifact.
- **Constructed loop turn.** @sec-running-the-loop demonstrates loop mechanics
  with explicitly assigned proxy and stronger-stage values. It does not report
  a SCALE-Sim, synthesis, or silicon result.
- **Runnable companion labs.** [Architecture 2.0 labs](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/tree/main/labs)
  are a separate executable practice path. The SCALE-Sim lab produces its own
  tool-backed observations and receipt; it neither reproduces nor replaces the
  constructed loop turn in @sec-running-the-loop.

## Living Registry Boundary

This appendix is not a general computer-architecture, pedagogy, or
reproducibility directory. Stable resource roles and adoption boundaries stay
here. Current tool, dataset, benchmark, and project links belong in the
searchable registry at
[arch2.mlsysbook.ai/tools/](https://arch2.mlsysbook.ai/tools/), where maintainers
can update or retire them without revising the book. Check versions before use
and cite the primary paper or project artifact for scholarly claims.

The [Architecture 2.0 GitHub repository](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2)
accepts registry contributions and executable artifacts. Submission to a
repository is not endorsement, independent evaluation, or adjudication. Those
claims require the governed institution or protocol described in the missing
infrastructure inventory.

## Using and Citing the Preview

Use this preview as a working vocabulary for paper reviews, project proposals,
class discussions, and internal design reviews. Cite the preview URL and version
when referring to this book, and cite the primary resource when making a claim
about a tool, dataset, benchmark, or method.
